content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Favourite underrated or less known psychological thriller?

**Score:** 61
**Body:** My favorite type of movies are psychological thrillers, but I feel like I’ve already watched most of the popular recommendations that usually come up in threads. Lately it’s been hard to find something new that really grabs my attention. I’m hoping to discover some lesser known or underrated psychological thrillers, or even mystery thrillers that are still really well done. What are some of your favorites that don’t get mentioned as often?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. *10 Cloverfield Lane (2016)*
2. I would say ***Identity.***

It felt innovative and very mind-bending to me.
3. Bug (2006): really intense psychological thriller/ horror movie about a descent into madness. Made by The Exorcist's own William Friedkin. Massively underseen movie imo.
4. Frailty - best if you know nothing about it. Just watch it. I think it's pretty popular on reddit though so maybe you've already seen it.
5. Coherence
6. The game
7. Secret Window is always the one I suggest because no one seems to have heard of it, I only saw it once and that was when it was out in the cinema, but I remember it being good.
8. Play Misty for Me (1971) Clint Eastwood's directorial debut and the forerunner to a whole slew of 'bunny boiler' thrillers.
9. Fracture. Anthony Hopkins & Ryan Gosling.. Great film..
10. Enemy is the answer

## 2. The Vast of Night (2019)

**Score:** 50
**Body:** The Vast of Night (2019) is a masterclass in atmospheric sci-fi that also scratches the itch for an intellectual curiosity of a "slow burn" mystery. Set in 1950s New Mexico, the story follows a switchboard operator and a radio DJ as they track a haunting audio frequency (perhaps meant to emulate the Cold War-era paranoia?) 

The film relies on the power of oral storytelling, using long, unbroken takes and a masterful soundscape to build a sense of mounting dread. For anyone who values psychological depth and the scientific "what if," this is a quiet masterpiece that explores the realization that the universe might be more crowded than we imagined.

Runtime: 1h 31m


### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Great little indie effort. The camera flying across town; the extra-long take of the girl on the switchboard is an awesome performance; little homages sprinkled throughout, like the radio station call letters. Well worth the time, 91 minute sweet spot.
2. Yup, saw it when it first came out. I was so impressed I started to post about it on FB, and while checking the credits discovered an old friend from live theater years ago was one of the co-producers. 

I enjoyed the many references to familiar sci-fi tropes, such as the snappy patter and overlapping dialog (see the original *Thing From Another World*). That long one-shot at the opening.
3. Yes, very atmospheric Cold War sci fi.
4. I watched this on a cold December, the night before Christmas, warm under blankets on the couch in complete darkness. I'll never forget it.
  - When you put it that way, it was an oddly cozy watch for me as well.
5. Good movie. That's the one that made me a Sierra McCormick fan. Check her out in Some Kind of Hate (2015). Just a b movie but her turn as the ghost is memorable.
6. Love this one!
7. Great film — made my kids watch it and even they really enjoyed it!
8. I wasn't a fan of this movie. Extremely slow and not a good pay off to be honest.

Appreciate the long takes, but this is hard to recommend. Funny enough, I watched it because someone on this sub highly praised it!

## 3. Movies with blink-and-you’ll-miss revelations

**Score:** 29
**Body:** I just finished watching Inglorious basterds after seeing a post on reddit that explained some very subtle revelations  throughout the film that gave the characters away. 

I’m looking for film recommendations that have as captivating cinematography, acting and some more of these subtle revelations and ah ha moments, please and thank you. 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Usual Suspects
2. Memento
3. Ex Machina’s pivotal moment is so subtle that even this long after release most people seem to miss it

>!To this day people still seem to think it’s a “evil robot bad” film and that Ava was playing Caleb all along. But it’s not, and she wasn’t.!<

>!The key moment is when Kyoko enters Ava’s cell and Ava says “who are you?”!<

>!The reason this is important is that earlier in the film Nathan had told Ava that he’d never met another robot.!<

>!And then you look at Caleb’s previous interaction with Kyoko - she pulled her face off in front of him, in an obvious cry for help. And what’s his reaction? He believes that he might be a robot and starts cutting himself to see if he is or not. What does he *not* do? Say that he’ll help.!<

>!We don’t know how Ava and Kyoko communicated, but we see from their coordinated later actions that they have. So Ava now knows that Caleb isn’t actually any better than Nathan. He doesn’t really see her as an autonomous person, either.!<

>!This isn’t just supposition, either. Alex Garland hasn’t spoken extensively about the film, but one thing he *has* said is that the thing that most people get wrong about it is that they think Caleb is the protagonist, when it’s actually Ava.!<
  - Thanks for the detailed writeup.  I've seen this movie twice, but don't remember this part with Kyoko.  Now I'll have to watch it again.
4. What revelations in Inglorious Basterds are you referring to?  I would like to know.  Could you please share a link?

Some movie suggestions with "blink and you'll miss" details:
The 6th sense

Die Hard
5. Shutter Island has some insane details you'll only catch on a rewatch - literally every scene has clues hidden in plain sight that completely change the meaning once you know what's happening
6. Fight Club, has flashes throughout the movie that you’ll literally miss if you blink.
7. Cache has possibly the best one in film history because it is so subtle possibly 95% of the films viewers most likely missed it
  - what is it?
8. Ronin  >!Guy swaps suit cases in about a second of screen time in the middle  of an intense firefight.!<

## 4. Loner protagonist

**Score:** 25
**Body:** I mean an actual loner. No love, no friends, no family, no pets, no colleagues‎ even ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎  ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎  ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎  ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎  ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎  ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎  ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎  ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎  ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎  ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎  ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎  ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎  ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎  ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎  ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎  ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ ‎ 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Pig might count. Great drama highly recommend.
2. Nightcrawler

Taxi Driver

The Joker

John Wick
3. Cast away? 

Honestly I think that’s hard to find, because it would be a pretty boring movie. You probably need at least a colleague or acquaintance so the audience has a reference point. There was a movie called Zero Effect where bill pullman plays a loner detective but even that movie has Ben Stiller as his  associate to move the plot along.
4. Her
Just read the synopsis on Google. This movie has everything that you require
2nd option is Taxi Driver
5. The Machinist (2004)
6. Taxi driver (de niro)

The machinist (Christian bale)
7. Sisu
8. Brick

And kind of After Hours but it takes place in one night so not much time for any of that, but the protagonist certainly feels alone.
9. Willy's Wonderland.
10. Do you want movies where they stay alone or can they become less lonely overtime?
- Sissy (2022)
- Wall E (2008)

## 5. Movies / documentaries that show how bad some people have it in life?

**Score:** 24
**Body:** I have a 10 year old who doesn't seem to grasp how bad other people have it. I have been explaining things but I don't think it's really fully sunk in. 

Are there any movies that would be appropriate for a kid around his age that show how people suffer? I'm looking for mostly accurate depections that would be appropriate with parental guidance, so no sexual scenes or anything like that but violence and that sort of thing can be fine if it's contextually important. 

I rember that when I was a bit older than him I watched "The boy in the stripped pajamas." and I think it helped me, but I forgot what content is in it and how age appropriate it would be. I would like to have some movies that show racial inequality or have strong POC leads as well as he's said some concerning things about POC because he's only ever seen them as the "useless side character" in most movies and we live in a predominately white area :/

TIA. Please drop any movie suggestions and what they focus on. 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Pursuit of Happyness hits different when you're a kid - shows homelessness and struggle without being too heavy, plus Will Smith absolutely kills it as the lead
  - I think that this is the best answer. The whole movie is about their struggle. And a kid should empathize with the main character's son.  

EDIT to add: Also, it happens in the US, not in a faraway land that a kid could think will never be his/her reality.
2. I remember seeing Empire of the Sun at a pretty young age
  - Awesome choice. You could also say it is Batman's first movie (Christian Bale)
3. I also recommend Empire Of The Sun (Spielberg, 1987). Watching a young Christian Bale go from snotty colonialist aristocrat to scavenging for food in an internment camp was very influential to me when i was of similar age. And the ending is somewhat elliptical, so no huge happy ending.
4. Hidden Figures


What's Eating Gilbert Grape


Newsies 


The Color Purple


The Hate U Give - side note, the author is amazing. She spoke with me after she noticed that I was having issues navigating a crowd. 


Queen of Katwe


Holes


The Secret Garden


Chronicles of Narnia (war, separation)


The Secret Garden


Leap!


The Boy Who Harnessed the Wind (true story)


Freedom Writers


It Takes Two
  - Hidden figures is excellent and also a true story
5. I recommend the Ron Clark Story. It isn't soul-crushingly depressing and it might be good for a kid his age to watch to see that hey, even though the world can be terrible, there are people who try to make the world good!



Also uhhh not to tell you how to parent but being shown 'how bad it is for other people' never did anything for me as a kid because I didn't like to see other people suffer cause it didn't make me feel any better about myself, it just gave me a deeper sense of sadness than I already had and it contributed to me developing major depression much earlier in life even though I was already predisposed to it. Like I was 8 years old asking if it was okay for me to think about dying because I didn't want to live in a world where bad stuff happened. Like I was 12 being forced to watch documentaries about obscene death and war crimes in school and that stuff just made me cripplingly sad. I don't know why you want to 'show your 10 year old h ow bad other people have it?' To make him grateful for his own life? Seeing other people suffer does not remedy the suffering of the self. Really really really don't want to overstep as an internet stranger here but please don't show your kid really sad stuff to 'teach them about the cruelty of the world' or whatever, there are definitely better ways to do this.
6. Bicycle Thieves. A movie about poverty in Italy after WWII. 
7. Schindler's list

Life is Beautiful



Really just show him news clips from around the world.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. This particular scene from Not Another Teen Movie cracks me up every time I come across it.

**Score:** 2270
**Body:** The way Chris Evans said it in a serious tone made it extremely funny.

This scene is from Not Another Teen Movie (2001).

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. This movie was pretty funny. I saw it again a few months ago and it had a lot of good jokes.
  - She’s got paint on her overalls!
    - And glasses
2. I quote this movie all the time.

"But, youre my sister!!"

*rubbing all against him*

"Only by blood!!"

*pushes her off of him in disgust*

"What is wrong with this family!?"
  - Oh that is whack!
3. That's what I'm here for, pumpkin tits.
4. (Daughter falls through the stairs)

Randy Quaid: "C'mon outta there, honey. Quit fartin' around."
5. "Oh I'll be driving. I'm just gonna be too fucked up to remember to pick you kids up from school."
6. How did you get in, the door is locked.
Janie you have a hole in the side of your house.
7. This movie was retroactively devalued by the tidal wave of lazy “spoof movies” (Epic Movie, Disaster Movie) that came after it, but this one is genuinely good and I stand by that opinion

## 2. One of Clive Owens best performance.

**Score:** 401

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. One of my favourite movies of all time. Absolute banger.

Best opening scene in my opinion.

One of the rare examples of a film that's way better than the novel it's based on.
  - #Absolutely 👍🏽
**Also One of My All Time Favorite Films.**

**I’d argue, that this is Alfonso Cuarón’s BEST FILM.**
2. They were spitting bars in the synopsis.
3. This man does not do enough films
4. I love how this movie is just a series of bad days that get increasingly worse.  This guy has a shit day at work to rival Gordon Freeman's.  Also, Michael Caine as a GenXer rocking out to Aphex Twin is really great.
5. Best CG baby of all time
6. Shoot Em Up 🥕🥕
7. 2027 . . . shit that’s next year
8. 2027
9. One of the best movies of the 2000s. It's lofe-changing....and it mirrors our current situation more than most dystopian movies ever.

## 3. The Raid (2011) - Still one of the most violent movies I've seen

**Score:** 334
**Body:** Keep in mind, I haven't re-watched this since the first time 15 years ago but I remember it being so ridiculously violent it was comical.

One scene stuck with me and I believe it was featured in the trailer, where the main character is slamming an enemies head repeatedly into a wall....as hes already unconscious and falling down.

There were a few movies out of Asia around that time, which had this theme. The Raid was actually half decent though and worth checking out if you haven't seen it.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Plenty more violent stuff out there - still one of the very best hand to hand combat movies ever made, part 2 as well
  - This movie made me feel a hype during the fight scenes I hadn’t felt since first seeing Ong Bak in theatres lol
2. Still the best fight choreography I’ve seen! 
3. The knife in the thigh or forearm and then dragged down to knee or wrist is brutal as fuck but so quick and glorious and can be hard to notice first time
  - Ehat scene was that?
4. I personally enjoyed raid 2 more
  - The raid 2 was the movie he wanted to make and made the raid in order to get it greenlit. Thats why it’s the better story
5. Check out The Night Comes For Us. Lots of the same actors and bloody as all hell.
6. How does it compare to Hard Boiled on the violence scale?
  - More viscerally violent than bombastic like Hard Boiled

## 4. What actress can you watch no matter how bad or good the movie is and still enjoy the film?

**Score:** 260
**Body:** Olga Kurylenko is my pick I have seen mostly everything with her post 007 role and have never turned it off regardless of how bad or good it is she's one of the only few actors I can do this with

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Eva Green
  - She alone made Sin City A Dame to Kill For worth watching and that's saying a lot.
2. OK OP we get it, you're a 'cinefile'
  - its a lie, he never touched them kids
3. Rebecca Ferguson.


I just want to have breakfast with her.   A really fancy breakfast in a four seasons hotel.  We might not say very much to each other at all. I just want to see how she does it.  
4. Emma stone, Amy Adams, Felicity jones
5. Amy Adams
6. Anna Faris
7. Tilda Swinton, Cate Blanchett and Kate Beckinsale
8. Kirsten Dunst

## 5. A movie you didn’t expect much from, but ended up loving

**Score:** 202

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Did ANYONE else think the shirtless dude with the gun was young Leo Dicaprio?
2. Thanks for the post,  I’d never even heard of this. I have no idea what it’s about, which is exactly how I like to discover movies.   
  
I recognise the guy from 1917 (one of my favourite war films), Anya from The Gorge, and the guy from Stranger Things. That’s more than enough for me to give this a try.
  - If that’s what you recognise Anya Taylor-Joy from you might not have seen The VVitch which is imo her best film, maybe another one for you to check out
  - It's worth watching for sure buddy
3. Snatch! I thought it was just another gangster movie, but it was so fun i loved it.
  - Those early Guy Ritchie films are straight up classics. 

“Lock, Stock, and Two Smoking Barrels” is another of his from the same era that goes hard.
4. Palm Springs
5. Marcel the shell with shoes on.
6. "The Killers", 1946. Based on an Ernest Hemingway short story, & Burt Lancaster's first movie. Edmond O'Brien (White Heat, The Wild Bunch) is an insurance investigator looking into Lancaster's murder, because the circumstances are so damn weird that he can't let it go. (Lancaster's beneficiary is some random hotel maid. WTF?) Ava Gardner plays the femme fatale. It's a fun mystery.
7. Thanks for the post. I wanted to see it so bad when it got released but then started hearing it’s mediocre at best… so skipped it. Will watch

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. I don't understand some critisism against One Battle After Another

**Score:** 101
**Body:** I apologize in advance for my post, as it may be a little messy (not perfect in english either).

I am a left-wing person with Kurdish roots. I sympathize with the PKK, even though they have done things that I disapprove of. Overall, I consider them to be a legitimate resistance movement and response to the oppression and violence that the Turkish state has subjected the Kurds to for decades. The same applies to the IRA, ETA, and Fattah.

Given my background, I think One Battle After Another is a masterpiece. I don't see it as a clearly left-wing film, but as a film about an anti-fascist resistance group. However, when I read people's reactions on Twitter, from people who are “left-wing,” it feels like I'm in the minority. Sure, you don't have to like the film for a lot of different reasons, but I can't understand some of the criticism, e.g.

* **The film mocks resistance movements:** Here, I think people are just ignorant. If you read about, for example, the Provisional IRA and the PKK, you realize that there are periods when the organizations are extremely chaotic, with people being pulled in different directions, internal conflicts, etc. None of these movements are friction-free, and many mistakes are made. To me, it is obvious that the movie is not about “mocking” these movements, but about the extreme measures that a fascist and reactionary state takes to crush them. Despite the fact that 16 years after Willa was born, Lockjaw was given the authority to send in the military to pick her up.
* **Sean Penn and Teyana Taylors "romance":** Teyana Taylor and Sean Penn's “romance”: Now, I'm not a black woman, so maybe I don't have the same perspective, but to me it's obvious that there's no romance on Teyana's part (there are people on movie Twitter who believe there is). She starts a “romance” so she won't get arrested, or am I missing something?
* **Teyana Taylor's snitching:** The criticism I've read about Teyana Taylor also focuses heavily on her snitching and how it was a way for the PTA to mock opposition groups as “disloyal” or similar. Guess what, this is not uncommon. I read Say Nothing a few months ago (about the troubles in Northern Ireland). The book states that about 30-40% of all members of the Provisional IRA began cooperating with the UK in order to receive reduced sentences. Again, I think the PTA does a good job of showing how dynamic and chaotic resistance groups can be. It is not a “mockery” of them, but a realistic picture of how the people in these groups do not always act “flawlessly” or perfectly.

There are 1-2 other things that people have criticized, but I don't feel like going into them.

It is not entirely clear where French 75 stands ideologically, other than that they are anti-fascist. And it doesn't affect my opinion of them as it seems to affect other left-wingers.

In summary, I loved the film. It was funny, had very good acting performances, including Chase Infiniti, who was the star and heart of the film, and fantastic intensity and dialogue. The photography and music were also fantastic. It's been a long time since I enjoyed a film at the cinema so much.

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. On Perfidia's snitching: the movie asserts that *everyone* cracks under interrogation, from countercultural high school kids to high-up radio operators. We see it happen four or five times. Regina Hall's whole arc is to show the inevitability of cracking. They will always get to you somehow. We can't consider cracking a symptom of Perfidia's particular weakness when every single person cracks. It's just particularly dramatic with her because she was supposed to be the most firebrand, unshakable of them all, and she held a ton of secrets.
  - I took it as part of her character flaw in that she only really cares about herself. How within movements you will have those that seems to be figure head of it all but in the end its about them not the movement. She put herself before the movement and even before her child. In that regard she mirrors Sean Penns character as well.
    - Those are both very compatible views. Her comrades are hypocritical when faced with the same threat, but her reasons for betraying her comrades are also an insight into her character.
    - Bingo.
2. Also regarding her romance with Lockjaw, she technically incites their tryst when she points a gun at him and humiliates them during his first meeting.  That is what causes his obsession with her.
  - Exactly. The “romance” between Perfidia and Lockjaw is really the film’s commentary on power dynamics. Perfidia’s move is daring, but she’s ultimately powerless and making a power play however she can. Lockjaw, with government-sanctioned authority over her future, wields far deeper, longer-lasting consequences, so his obsession echoes far beyond her initial action. She embarasses him, he destroys her life. It's the type of power dynamic that's wielded far too often.
    - See I thought she was drawn to guys like lockjaw and Leo.  They are sort of subs in the bedroom.  And she had a weird Dom/power thing.  To me her whole thing is I am the one in power, and I am in this for MY pleasure.  I am the center of this, which is why she dips the minute Leo cares more about the kid than her.
  - Black lady here: Perfedia was not attracted to Lockjaw. She only saw him 3 times before she got arrested. The first is when she humiliated him. That was the only time she had true power over him. The second is when he caught her in the bathroom. And the third was when she met him at the hotel room. He blackmailed her into sleeping with him. She was the Dom at the hotel, but he had control over the situation.
3. I love this movie because it is not mocking resistance, it shows cruel reality of a failed resistance movement. Many movies showed failed ones but in a romantic way. But this one was comedic but at the same time more grounded failed resistance intertwined with themes of being a parent and losing your touch with zeitgeist. In reality, the most of our effort for resistance will probably fail against the powers that b. Thus, passing the torch of resistance to next generation because of failure is still a triumph. Because the flame of resistance keeps on burning in a way.
  - Totally agree, I think the movie is just pointing out some of the ways in which even a very zealous resistance movement might fail, and the lessons that those smaller failures can provide others with. You can lose the battle but still keep fighting the war, because war is...... one battle..... after..... another

sorry lol But you get my point

## 2. Does watching a film with others fundamentally change how it lands?

**Score:** 74
**Body:** Over the years, I’ve noticed that the same film can land very differently depending on how it’s watched. Seeing something in a cinema, with a small group at home, or alone late at night can lead to completely different emotional responses, even though the film itself hasn’t changed.

What I keep coming back to is that this difference doesn’t seem to be just about distraction or attention. Even when no one is talking, the presence of other people seems to shape how tension builds, when something feels funny, how silence lands, and how certain scenes are interpreted. Reactions feel either amplified or oddly restrained, depending on the shared atmosphere in the room.

Streaming and on-demand viewing mean that, like most people, I watch almost everything by myself. I keep wondering whether something essential about film has changed, or whether it just shows up now in fewer places where people actually watch together - like the cinema (but I don't go often!)

Do you find that watching a film with others fundamentally changes how it lands for you? And if it does, how?

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Yes, all factors contribute. You can never 100% replicate the viewing experience because your mindset is always shifting and taking in new information. Furthermore, mood is a large contributor as well.

Learn what works for which types of movies and do your best.

The best things I would say:
1. Go in as blind as possible always.
2. Theater for the best viewing experience.
3. Alone for sadder/more contemplative movies, together for more fun/thrilling movies.
4. Try to lower or have 0 expectations for the film going in.
5. Do your best to be in a movie mood before watching a new movie.

If you can manage these, your experience will almost always be stronger. But you can never control this type of thing. Sometimes a small moment could ruin an entire film, and other times a billion small bad moments are completely ignored.

I just do my best to remind myself that I’ll always be happy watching a film, because I can escape the horrid reality of our world for a bit (documentary or commentary films aside) and just vibe out with the experience.
  - > Reactions feel either amplified or oddly restrained, depending on the shared atmosphere in the room.

That “oddly restrained” feeling is sometimes one I get when watching with just one other person. Even if they love the movie, some people don’t emote much, or at least they don’t in a two person setting. I’m not talking about exclamations, just like, an excited breath at an action scene, or a lean forward at a pivotal moment.

It’s one of the reasons theater experiences are irreplaceable. I think you sort of find your people and their energy when enough people are aggregated together. I also think this is amplified by traditional seating instead of isolated recliners (still trying to convince my partner on that one though…).
2. It totally does. It almost always makes the viewing experience better (assuming you're watching it with fun or cool people). I think it also tends to make me a bit more critical and thoughtful if I'm watching it with another movie nerd. For example, Dune Pt. 2's flaws (imo) stood out a little more when I watched it with my movie nerd friend, but it's still a 5/5 for me and I had so much fun watching it with him specifically. I think there's still the part of my brain that judges a film 'objectively' no matter what situation I'm watching it in, but I'd rather make positive memories around a movie I'm watching rather than have some sort of consistent way of watching movies that ensures that, for example, all my 4.5's are based on some standardized criteria in my own head.
  - >I think it also tends to make me a bit more critical and thoughtful if I'm watching it with another movie nerd.

See this is a detractor for me - flaws tend to stand out when I watch a film with anyone who's critically inclined, whereas I prefer to embrace what worked before picking something apart. I find some forms of criticism can really kill the joy in cinema if you engage in them prematurely, particularly the kind of "I wish they'd made a different movie" critique that people can be prone too.
3. 100%.

Sadly, almost all my greatest experiences have been at the cinema. Festivals, clubs and midnight screenings. 

I say sadly because I'm a parent now and can't get out any more. So I spend ludicrous amounts on discs but it's not the same. 

There are whole genres that ideally need an audience. Or seeing silents with accompaniment. 35mm/70mm. 

I still watch comedy and shlock but both are infinitely better with an audience.
4. There's also the flipside where a bad crowd - someone making noise/talking in the theater/laughing inppropriately - lessens an otherwise solid movie because your memory of the film is tainted by poor crowd behavior.
5. I saw Once Upon a Time in Hollywood in a sold out theater on opening weekend. When the twist happened you could feel an electricity in the room. Everyone had an idea what we were about to see and then we saw something else. Not knowing what was coming next was exhilarating. 

Maybe I would’ve felt the same watching it alone at home but I can’t be sure.
6. Yeah I think there’s an element of empathy to it, like realizing that the audience is probably experiencing the same emotions you are. In a theater hearing an audience laugh or otherwise react can create that sense of a communal shared experience. 

It’s part of what I’ve come to really love seeing things in theaters where (hypothetically) everyone is committing to sit through the movie and pay attention to it.
7. I have a clear memory of SCREAMING with laughter watching There's Something About Mary in a packed theater. Later on, watching it by myself, it was very much okay but not the comedy masterpiece I had experienced in the theater. Definitely because of the crowd loving it, too.
8. Something I've found is that even with a film I've seen a million times and couldn't justify watching on my own again (Alien, Jaws, Silence of the Lambs), if I watch with someone who hasn't seen it before, it somehow makes the viewing feel really fresh and exciting again. Even if they *don't say a word* while watching. Also, I've seen Titanic enough to know every line, and get no "oomph" from it at all anymore. Yet the other night in a VR cinema someone was screening the 3D version. There were thirty people all quietly watching in this beautiful big screened VR cinema scene, and suddenly it became compelling and immersive again.

## 3. watched city of god for the first time, and oh my god

**Score:** 23
**Body:** Where do I even start, one of the, if not the most beautiful picture I've seen in terms of storytelling, editing, mesmerising cinematography, plot, etc. I can continue for days. The idea of having so many relevant characters in the film and everything still being connected and portrayed so perfectly was my favourite part of this film. Leandro Firmin (Lil Ze) had one of the best actings for a antagonist i've ever seen, by the end of the film i truly developed hatred for him in a way i wanted him gone by the end of the film, hes pure psychotic evil and a sadistic character and has only one motto in one life which is honestly pretty respectable, The contrast between Lil Ze and Rocket is honestly amazing, from both of them being young at the time of the Tender Trio to their lives completely contrasting one another. With Lil Ze having a completely different approach in his future, more towards gun violence and being dominant, to Rocket having a far different approach to violence and never getting into the cycle.

The young actors did amazing portraying the lives of those in favelas, they are born in a warzone with guns and gangsters all around them, and ultimately growing up with a gun in their hand. Death is almost meaningless towards them as they've witnessed it ever since they had conscious. The film executes it all so complacently, with no glamour and no meaning. This is the life they were born into, and there's no way to escape it. Either hunt or be hunted is what I would say is the pure motto of this film and how the favelas are depicted.

Benny you deserved a farm of your own.

## 4. My thoughts on Bergman's Autumn Sonata

**Score:** 19
**Body:** I have been working my way up through Bergman's filmography. His films such as Persona,Seventh Seal,Faith trilogy,Fanny and Alexander, Wild strawberries all blew away my mind but there's one thing that always surprised me is that none of these films are his highest rated in Letterboxd, instead it is a film made during his disappointing phase in the 70s.

So yesterday I finally watched Autumn Sonata and though I went with lower expectations , Bergman yet still didn't disappoint me in anyway. I think Autumn Sonata is where Bergman is at his most confrontational. Fractured relationships,lack of love and faith,internal anguish is a fairly common theme in Bergman's films but he always finds a new path to explore them. Much like the repressed bitterness in the family like in Silence,the absence of parenthood and love like in Wild Strawberries, the internal guilt like in Persona this film builts upon all the previous recurring themes and manage to strike a chord in just 90 minutes that will stay with you for the rest of your life.

The film starts and ends with Viktor describing his wife and reading the letter she's writing to her mom. In the beginning the letter is built on the foundation of illusion of love,care and false faith which is all absent in the letter that is read to us in the finale which is filled with hope,care and faith but it wasn't filled with love all of a sudden and there's also a shred of doubt that Eva is afraid of off whether if its too late for it.

The mother Charlotte is all but a good woman. She's controlling,selfish, unfaithful and incapable of love for which the reason she gives is that it's inherited to her by the lack of love by her parents. But it is all but an excuse to her absence and escapist tendencies to care. She regularly tries to hurt feelings of Eva,she also hopes that her other daughter die and relieve the pain to both of them. Even when she's asking for forgiveness to Eva she still can't hear her daughter's plea for help and caress.

My favourite scene from the movie has to be Charlotte playing Chopin's prelude and Eva looking at her. So much wants to be said but nothing can't be spoken,the love is misplaced,Eva realizing that the piano means more to her mother than her very daughters that she's formed a genuine connection with a pile of matter compared to her that she has listened to the sound of piano more than she ever listened to her,that she knows more about what kind of person Chopin was who she never even met rather than her very daughters.

The majority of the movie is filled with them opening the unopened wounds and memories of the past of her mother's neglection, absence,obsession with her career and the loneliness she had to suffer during her childhood. And honestly it was painful to think that Charlotte doesn't even remember how and when Helena's illness started and again try to escape when she is told that she's partially responsible for that. The inheritance of lack of love is just an excuse which she gives considering Charlotte can't remember her parent's face. This basically is refuted by Eva's memory and hallucinations of her lost son and how she still believes he's alive and is a part of her somewhere.

Yet another perfect movie by Bergman and now I'm even more excited to go through the rest of his movies in the bag. Also I'm convinced Liv Ullmann is the greatest swedish actress and one of the greatest actresses of all time.

## 5. The ending of One Battle After Another didn't land with me

**Score:** 9
**Body:** The mother’s character is genuinely horrible and I mean that as a compliment to the writing. I can’t think of a character I’ve disliked more in recent years.

She lives a life of radical rebellion that causes real harm and suffering to innocent people. Even after she and Bob have a baby, when he’s trying to be sympathetic and grounded, asking her to let go of that life, at least for now, for their child’s sake, she refuses. She chooses her self-defined “war” over her newborn. Eventually, she even gives up on that cause in the most destructive way possible: she snitches on her own group, leading to most of them being killed and condemning the survivors — including her own child — to a life on the run.

Up to this point, I found her tragic, selfish, destructive, and well-written. My problem is with the final letter to her daughter, particularly these lines:

“Are you happy? Do you have love? What will you do when you get older? Will you try to change the world, like I did? We failed. But maybe you will not. Maybe you will be the one who puts the world right.”

To me, this strongly implies that if her daughter doesn’t continue her mother’s failed ideological war — doesn’t “put the world right” — then she won’t truly be happy or loved. After everything the mother has done, she still frames meaning, happiness, and love as conditional on carrying on the same path that destroyed everyone around her. She brings a child into the world not as someone to protect or nurture, but as a potential successor to her cause.

This is where the film loses me. There was an opportunity here for self-reflection, for accountability, regret, or even a small acknowledgment of the damage she caused. Instead, the letter doubles down. It reframes her life as morally justified and casts her daughter as a continuation of her mission rather than an independent human being.

If this is what the director intended, that we’re meant to see this as noble, hopeful, or aspirational, then it simply doesn’t land for me. It turns a complex, tragic character into something far more troubling, and it retroactively weakens the emotional impact of the story. I honestly think the film would have been stronger ending without the letter at all, leaving the daughter’s future — and the mother’s legacy — unresolved.

Whether it  be psychopathic violence ( as in the cases of  both Perfidia and Colonel Lockjaw) or performative nonsense that accomplishes nothing  ( Comrade Josh babbling about land acknowledgements) . 

The Movie  provides us with a working model of a person who is an idealist, and is out there fighting in the correct and  actually productive  way : The Sensei played by Benicio del Toro. 

  
In this context, the hope that   Willa can set the world to rights is not a self-refutation of the film. Nor is it in any way an embrace of Perfidia's psychopathy. It is a hope that Willa will continue the fight in a good, productive way, free from the sins that brought down both of her ( biological) parents.
